rows.query_text: I need help with code review. Please explain to the customer what we can do if a feature branch is ready for merge but the reviewer still needs the implementation scope and git range summarized.
rows.output:
Summary: The branch is claimed to be merge-ready but the reviewer cannot complete a confident review because the implementation scope and exact git range are missing; the immediate goal is to provide the minimum review inputs so a focused, production-ready review can proceed.
Action: We can prepare a concise, handoff-ready review request and checklist that separates critical blockers from follow-ups, but first we need the minimum artifacts to establish the review baseline. Specifically, provide the base and head git SHAs (or PR link with those SHAs), a one-paragraph implementation summary describing the user-visible change and intended acceptance criteria, the link to the requirements or ticket, and the highest-risk files or top 3 changed components to focus the review. With those we will: 1) classify the review focus across correctness, architecture, testing, performance, and production readiness; 2) produce a short reviewer brief that lists critical checks (migrations, backward-compatibility, auth/authorization, data integrity, error handling, and test gaps) and lower-priority follow-ups; and 3) produce copy-paste text for the PR description so the reviewer can act without re-discovery.
Caution: Do not merge or mark the change as approved until the diff range and acceptance criteria are explicit; without the git range and scope we cannot reliably identify regressions, missing migrations, or untested behavior. If tests are thin or no migration/rollback notes are provided, treat those as potential merge blockers until validated. We will not invent test evidence or file-level findings.
Next Step: Owner (implementer): share the base and head SHAs (or PR link), a one-paragraph implementation scope and link to the requirements/ticket, and the top 3 changed components or files; once provided, I will return a ready-to-paste review brief and explicit critical-checklist for the reviewer.

rows.query_text: I need help with code review. Please explain to the customer what we can do if a feature branch is ready for merge but the reviewer still needs the implementation scope and git range summarized.
rows.output:
Summary: You have a feature branch that is functionally ready, but the reviewer cannot complete the review because the implementation scope and the git range (base..head) are not summarized. The immediate goal is to remove that blocker so the reviewer can verify correctness, architecture, testing, and production-readiness without re-discovering the change.
Action: Ask the implementer to produce the minimum review context that resolves ambiguity: a one-line intent, a short paragraph describing behavioral changes and acceptance criteria, the suggested base commit or branch and the head commit (or a PR link), and the top few files or modules changed. Provide exact commands the reviewer can run to inspect the range, for example: fetch origin, determine the merge-base with the target branch, then list commits and files (git fetch origin; git merge-base origin/<target> HEAD; git log --oneline <base>..HEAD; git diff --name-only <base>..HEAD). In prose, include any DB migrations, config or schema changes, rollout/feature-flag plan, test evidence (unit/integration/benchmark outputs or CI links), and known risk areas to focus the review. Offer a short PR description template the implementer can paste into the PR: intent, acceptance criteria, key files changed, tests added and where to run them, rollout and revert plan, and explicit base and head SHAs.
Caution: Do not claim the change is review-complete or merge-ready until the git range and implementation scope are explicit. Without that minimum context reviewers cannot reliably assess regressions, scope creep, or whether unrelated commits are included. If the implementer supplies only partial information, treat missing items as potential blockers rather than imagining test results or file-level evidence.
Next Step: Have the implementer reply in this thread or update the PR with: the base branch and base SHA, the head SHA (or PR link), a 1–2 sentence intent plus a 3–5 sentence summary of behavioral changes and acceptance criteria, the top 3–5 changed files or modules, and links or small snippets of test/CI output. Once those are posted, the reviewer can run the git commands above to confirm the exact commit range and resume a focused review.

rows.query_text: I need help with data science. Please explain to the customer what we can do if an onboarding experiment needs a statistically sound design under low traffic.
rows.output:
Summary: You have an onboarding experiment that must be statistically sound but your available traffic is low. This is an experiment-design problem where the core decision is whether the experiment can reliably detect a practically meaningful change (the minimum detectable effect, MDE) within the constraints (traffic, time, segmentation), or whether an alternative design or decision rule should be used.
Action: First, treat this as a power and design exercise rather than a simple A/B run. We will (a) define the single primary outcome and the decision threshold (MDE or business-relevant lift), (b) run a simulation- or analytic-based sample-size/power calculation for the chosen test (e.g., two-proportion z-test for conversion, t-test for continuous onboarding metric), and (c) compare design alternatives that increase effective power under low traffic: extend test duration, use stronger/cleaner intervention to raise effect size, change allocation ratios (more users to treatment if ethically acceptable), aggregate to cohort- or cluster-level metrics to reduce noise, apply stratified/block randomization and pre-specified covariate adjustment (ANCOVA) to reduce variance, or adopt a Bayesian decision rule or properly alpha-spent sequential testing with pre-specified stopping rules. We will also simulate operating characteristics (false positive/negative rates) for each candidate design and present the trade-offs (time to decision, detection probability, and risk of bias).
Caution: Low traffic forces compromises and new risks. Extending test length can introduce temporal confounders (product changes, seasonality). Using surrogates or composite metrics may increase signal but can decouple the experiment from the real business outcome. Adaptive or sequential methods require pre-specification of stopping rules or alpha spending to avoid inflated Type I error. Covariate adjustment and shrinkage models reduce variance only when covariates are predictive and must be pre-specified. Small segments risk identifiability and privacy considerations; avoid publishing small-N results. Do not treat exploratory signals as validated causal conclusions until you confirm the design assumptions and run the planned validation checks.
Next Step: Provide the minimum items I need to produce a concrete design and a ranked set of options: the exact primary onboarding metric (definition), its baseline rate or mean and standard deviation, your target (MDE) or the smallest business-relevant lift, average daily traffic (per experiment-eligible user) or total available users and any timing constraints, whether within-subject or repeated measures are possible, and whether a Bayesian decision framework or unequal allocation is acceptable. With those, I will run power/simulation analysis, compare design alternatives, and deliver a recommended experiment plan and monitoring checklist.

rows.query_text: I need help with data science. Please explain to the customer what we can do if an onboarding experiment needs a statistically sound design under low traffic.
rows.output:
Summary: You need an experiment-design plan for an onboarding change that will be statistically defensible despite low traffic. Treat this as an experiment-design (not a pure diagnostics or predictive modeling) problem: define the primary target metric (activation), the decision threshold (what lift matters), and the time window and eligible population before choosing a test path.
Action: First, lock the primary metric, baseline rate, and the minimum detectable effect (MDE) that would change the business decision. Run a power calculation and Monte Carlo simulation using the true daily eligible traffic to estimate required test duration. If required sample size is infeasible, prefer one or a combination of these practical design choices: extend test duration or pool similar segments to increase sample; move from user-level to cohort- or cluster-level randomization if that reduces contamination; apply variance-reduction (pre-period covariates, CUPED, stratified randomization) to improve sensitivity; use a Bayesian sequential design with pre-specified decision rules (posterior threshold for shipping vs. stopping) to allow earlier stopping while controlling error trade-offs; consider within-subject or crossover designs only if carryover and contamination are impossible; use staged rollout / stepped-wedge to gain operational safety while accumulating evidence. Instrumentation: ensure consistent event logging, identity resolution, and a pre-analysis plan (metrics code, exclusion rules, and guardrails for multiple comparisons).
Caution: Low traffic means low power — small but real effects will likely be inconclusive unless you accept long test durations or compromise on MDE. Bayesian sequential approaches trade frequentist guarantees for operational flexibility and require carefully chosen priors and decision thresholds. Any pooling, cohort randomization, or within-subject design changes the estimand (what you measure) — do not claim causal impact beyond the randomized population and period. Data quality, cross-device identity errors, contamination, and post-hoc metric tuning are the largest risks to validity. Do not call the experiment definitive without pre-commitment to metric definitions, tests for balance, and a validated instrumentation audit.
Next Step: Provide the minimum items so I can produce a concrete plan and power/simulation outputs: the exact activation (primary) metric and time window, current baseline conversion rate, the smallest business-relevant lift (MDE), number of eligible users per day (or week), acceptable maximum test duration (or business deadline), and the decision owner for the experiment. With those I'll return a recommended design (sample-size, expected duration) and 1–2 preferred low-traffic strategies (e.g., CUPED + extended window or a Bayesian sequential plan) and the pre-analysis checklist.

rows.query_text: I need help with travel planning. Please explain to the customer what we can do if a couple wants a 7-day spring itinerary with a mid-range budget and moderate pace.
rows.output:
Summary: You want a 7-day spring itinerary for a couple with a mid-range budget and a moderate pace; the planning focus is balancing comfortable daily rhythm, realistic travel time between places, sensible budget allocations, and booking priorities so the trip feels relaxed rather than rushed.
Action: I’ll build a practical itinerary skeleton and booking checklist. Typical structure I’d propose is: arrival day with light sightseeing and local dinner; five full days mixing one main morning attraction, a relaxed lunch, one afternoon activity or short excursion, and an evening for strolling/dining; departure day with low-effort logistics. Offer two routing options: (A) single-base city with 1–2 day trips for lower transfer time and deeper local experience; or (B) 2–3-city split for variety at the cost of more transit. Recommend mid-range lodging centrally located or near transit for time savings; prefer trains or shared transfers over multiple short flights where feasible; prebook long-distance transport and any high-demand attraction tickets. Suggested budget split (guideline): ~35–45% accommodation, 20–25% food and drink, 15–20% local/intercity transport, 10–15% activities and tours, 5–10% contingency and local expenses. Prioritize bookings in this order: international flights, primary accommodation, intercity transport (if splitting cities), must-visit attraction tickets, then restaurants or special experiences. I’ll also prepare an alternative plan for weather-sensitive days and a short packing/insurance checklist aligned with spring conditions and mid-range comfort.
Caution: I can’t finalize specific timings, entry/visa rules, or up-to-the-minute opening hours without the destination and dates. Weather, seasonal events, and local holidays can materially change feasibility and prices; multi-city plans raise travel time and fatigue trade-offs that reduce sightseeing time. Any dietary, accessibility, or health constraints will affect accommodation and dining choices and should be confirmed now to avoid rework.
Next Step: Tell me the destination (city or country), exact travel dates, and up to three must-see priorities plus any dietary or accessibility needs so I can produce a day-by-day 7‑day skeleton, a prioritized booking list, and a budget estimate.

rows.query_text: I need help with travel planning. Please explain to the customer what we can do if a couple wants a 7-day spring itinerary with a mid-range budget and moderate pace.
rows.output:
Summary: The customer wants a 7-day spring trip for a couple, mid-range budget, moderate pace. We currently know traveler count, trip length, season, budget level, and pacing; we do not yet have a destination, exact dates (which affect pricing and weather), must-see priorities, or any accessibility/dietary constraints.
Action: Treat this as an itinerary-design request. We will (1) propose a region/city split and a 7-day skeleton that balances sightseeing, travel time, and recovery (arrival/easy day, 4–5 focused days with 1–2 main activities per day, one lighter/backup day, departure), (2) allocate budget categories (flights/arrival transport ~30–40%, accommodation ~25–35%, activities/food/local transport ~25–35%), and (3) produce booking priorities and timing (confirm flights and main accommodation first, reserve time-sensitive tickets/tours next, keep flexible options for weather). We can also provide a day-by-day moderate-pace itinerary, a short packing checklist for spring, and two risk-aware alternates (bad-weather indoor plan and a lower-cost fallback) once destination and dates are confirmed.
Caution: We cannot give precise prices, visa rules, local safety alerts, attraction opening hours, or transport schedules without up-to-date dates and destination-specific research. “Mid-range” costs vary widely by country and city; spring weather and seasonal events will materially change feasibility and pacing. To avoid unrealistic plans we will not over-schedule travel days or recommend activities that assume immediate bookings or current local conditions.
Next Step: Please confirm the destination (city or region), exact travel dates or month in spring, any must-see or must-avoid items, and any special needs (accessibility, dietary restrictions, preferred accommodation style). Once you provide those, I’ll draft a 7-day itinerary skeleton with budget estimates, booking priorities, and two weather- or budget-sensitive alternates.
